820	### The orchestrator skeleton (`vending_service.py`)
821	
822	🎙️ Open: *"Here's the brain — it just holds working memory and routes each action to the current state."*
823	
824	```python copy
825	class VendingService(VendingContext):
826	    def __init__(self, slots: dict[str, Slot], coin_bank: CoinBank) -> None:
827	        self._slots = slots
828	        self._coin_bank = coin_bank
829	        self._inserted: list[Coin] = []
830	        self._selected_code: str | None = None
831	        self._state: MachineState = IdleState()      # boots in IDLE
832	
833	    def insert_coin(self, coin: Coin) -> int: ...
834	    def select_product(self, code: str) -> None: ...
835	    def dispense(self) -> DispenseResult: ...
836	    def cancel(self) -> tuple[Coin, ...]: ...
837	    def refill(self, code: str, units: int) -> None: ...
838	
839	    @contextmanager
840	    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
841	        yield                                         # no-op; subclass overrides
842	```
843	
844	Now the public methods — each just wraps the (no-op) lock and forwards to the current state, adopting whatever state it returns:
845	
846	```python copy
847	def insert_coin(self, coin: Coin) -> int:
848	    with self._lock():
849	        self._state = self._state.insert_coin(self, coin)
850	        return self.inserted_cents()
851	
852	def select_product(self, code: str) -> None:
853	    with self._lock():
854	        self._state = self._state.select(self, code)
855	
856	def dispense(self) -> DispenseResult:
857	    with self._lock():
858	        self._state, result = self._state.dispense(self)
859	        return result
860	
861	def cancel(self) -> tuple[Coin, ...]:
862	    with self._lock():
863	        self._state, refund = self._state.cancel(self)
864	        return refund
865	```
866	
867	> **Reasoning:** The service is deliberately thin. It owns the data (via the `VendingContext` properties) and does the routing; all the phase rules live in the state classes. The `self._state = self._state.<action>(...)` line is the State pattern in one expression — adopt whatever next state the current state hands back. `dispense` unpacks a tuple because it alone also produces output.
868	
869	`refill` and the helper:
870	
871	```python copy
872	def refill(self, code: str, units: int) -> None:
873	    with self._lock():
874	        slot = self._slots.get(code)
875	        if slot is None:
876	            raise InvalidSelectionError(f"no such product code: {code}")
877	        slot.refill(units)
878	```
879	
880	#### Approach — locating + validating a selectable slot (`_validate_selectable`)
881	
882	**Approach 1 — validate only in `select`, trust it in `dispense` (rejected):**
883	```
884	select(code): assert slots[code] in stock; remember code
885	dispense():   slot = slots[selected]; vend it      # trusts select-time stock
886	```
887	> **Challenges:** stock can go stale between select and dispense — a refill, or a concurrent buyer, empties the slot. `dispense` then vends a now-empty slot → negative stock. State divergence.
888	
889	**Approach 2 — keep a separate `available_codes` set kept in sync (rejected for this scale):**
890	```
891	on vend / refill: maintain a set of in-stock codes; select/dispense check the set
892	```
893	> **Challenges:** the set is a second source of truth — forget to update it on one path and a slot vanishes from availability or sells while empty. For a single machine with a handful of slots, this synchronisation risk buys nothing.
894	
895	**Chosen — one shared guard hit at BOTH select and dispense:**
896	```
897	_validate_selectable(ctx, code):
898	    slot = slots.get(code); if None -> InvalidSelectionError
899	    if not slot.is_in_stock() -> OutOfStockError
900	    return slot
901	```
902	> **Why it wins:** single source of truth — the slot's own `quantity`. Both `select` (fast reject) and `dispense` (moment of truth) call the SAME guard, so the rules can't drift. O(1) dict lookup; no second structure to keep in sync.
903	
904	### Verification — full-lifecycle dry runs
905	
906	**Scenario 1 — Happy path (buy cola with $1.00):**
907	Initial: A1 qty=3, `inserted=[]`, `selected_code=None`, state=IDLE.
908	
909	| Step | Call | After |
910	|---|---|---|
911	| 1 | `insert_coin(QUARTER)` ×4 | `inserted=[Q,Q,Q,Q]`, state=COLLECTING_MONEY, balance 100 |
912	| 2 | `select_product("A1")` | `selected_code="A1"` |
913	| 3 | `dispense()` | A1 qty 3→2, returns Cola + (QUARTER,), `inserted=[]`, `selected_code=None`, state=IDLE |
914	
915	Both working-memory fields cleared, slot decremented, change correct. ✓
916	
917	**Scenario 2 — Can't make change (reclaim cleanly):**
918	Bank empty, B1 (Chips, 70c) qty=1. Insert DOLLAR, select B1.
919	
920	| Step | Call | After |
921	|---|---|---|
922	| 1 | `insert_coin(DOLLAR)` | `inserted=[$]`, state=COLLECTING_MONEY |
923	| 2 | `select_product("B1")` | `selected_code="B1"` |
924	| 3 | `dispense()` | deposits $, `make_change(30)` fails → rolls $ back out, raises `CannotMakeChangeError`; B1 qty still 1; bank unchanged |
925	| 4 | `cancel()` | refunds (DOLLAR,), state=IDLE |
926	
927	The failed sale never vended, never kept the money. ✓
928	
929	**Scenario 3 — Race (plain English):** Two threads buy the last gum. WITHOUT a lock both pass the in-stock re-check in `dispense` and both call `vend_one`, driving qty to -1 (double-dispense) — the `vend_one` backstop catches the second only if it happens to run after the first fully committed, which under a widened critical section it does not. WITH the §9 lock, the whole transaction is atomic: thread A holds the lock through check+vend, thread B waits, then sees qty 0 and gets `OutOfStockError`. Exactly one wins; stock ends at 0.

932	
933	| Operation | Time | Space | Key reason |
934	|---|---|---|---|
935	| `insert_coin` | O(1) | O(1) | append to list + sum of a tiny list |
936	| `select_product` | O(1) | O(1) | dict lookup |
937	| `dispense` | O(1) | O(1) | dict lookup + O(5) make-change over fixed denominations |
938	| `cancel` | O(k) | O(k) | snapshot the k inserted coins (k small) |
939	| `refill` | O(1) | O(1) | dict lookup + increment |

1006	
1007	> 🎙️ **Walk it arrow by arrow:** "The customer calls `dispense` on the service. The service doesn't do the work — it forwards to the current state, `HasMoneyState`. The state asks the `Slot` for stock and price, checks the paid amount covers it, then deposits the customer's coins into the `CoinBank` and asks it for exact change. The interesting moment is `make_change` returning a quarter — that's the all-or-nothing change. Then `vend_one` on the slot is the atomic decrement that guards the race. The state hands back `IdleState` plus the result; the service stores the new state and returns the product."
1008	
1009	**Why the sequence shows the design is good:** notice the service coordinates but implements zero business logic — every decision delegates to a state, a slot, or the bank. That's Information Expert and Open/Closed at work: a new phase or a new change algorithm slots in without touching this flow.

1013	## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
1014	
1015	**DEFAULT: describe verbally. Write code only if asked.**
1016	
1017	🗣️ **Step 1 — Name the race:** "In `dispense`, two button-press threads on the same machine both re-check 'gum in stock', both see qty 1, both call `vend_one`. Stock goes to -1 — double-dispense. It's a check-then-act race: the stock check and the decrement aren't one atomic step. The same threads also stomp on the shared inserted-coins list and the state pointer."
1018	
1019	🗣️ **Step 2 — The solution:** "I guard the *whole transaction* with one reentrant lock on the machine. Before `dispense` (and every public method) does anything, it acquires the lock; it releases at the end. Only one customer's transaction runs at a time."
1020	
1021	🗣️ **Step 3 — The trade-off:** "On a single machine this is exactly right — there's no per-slot parallelism to win because every action shares the same inserted-coins tally and the same coin bank. The machine itself is the unit of mutual exclusion. A finer lock would be pure complexity for zero throughput gain."
1022	
1023	🗣️ **Step 4 — Distributed (if asked):** "A fleet of machines would each run their own process and their own lock — no shared memory, so no cross-machine contention by design. If they shared a central inventory you'd push atomicity to the DB: `UPDATE slot SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE code = ? AND qty > 0` — a compare-and-set; whoever's update affects 0 rows lost and gets OutOfStock."
1024	
1025	**Concurrency Vocabulary**
1026	
1027	| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
1028	|---|---|---|
1029	| Race condition | Two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | `dispense` on the last unit |
1030	| Critical section | The block that must run as one atomic step | re-check stock → make change → `vend_one` |
1031	| Check-then-act | Check a condition, then act, but state changed in between | "saw qty 1, then vended" |
1032	| Atomic | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the lock gives the whole transaction |
1033	| Mutex / lock | A token only one thread holds at a time | `threading.RLock` on the machine |
1034	| Reentrant (RLock) | Same thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | a guarded method may call another guarded path |
1035	| Lock granularity | How much one lock covers | whole-MACHINE (one machine = one unit) |
1036	| Pessimistic locking | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `RLock` |
1037	| Optimistic locking | Don't lock; write conditionally, retry on loss | the DB compare-and-set, distributed case |
1038	| Compare-and-set | Atomic "update only if value still X" | `WHERE qty > 0` |
1039	| Deadlock | Two threads each hold what the other needs | avoided: only ever ONE lock held |
1040	| Starvation | A thread never gets the lock | not a risk; the critical section is microseconds |
1041	
1042	**Concurrency Follow-up Q&A**
1043	
1044	> ❓ "Why `RLock` and not a plain `Lock`?"
1045	> "Because a guarded public method can call another path that the same thread also runs under the lock. A plain `Lock` would deadlock on the second acquire by the same thread; an `RLock` lets the owning thread re-enter and only fully releases when the outermost `with` exits."
1046	
1047	> ❓ "Why one machine-wide lock instead of one per slot?"
1048	> "Per-slot locking would let two buyers vend two different slots in parallel — but on a single machine they still share ONE inserted-coins tally and ONE coin bank. So a per-slot lock wouldn't actually let them proceed independently; they'd corrupt the shared money state. The machine is the real unit of contention, so a single lock is both correct and simplest."
1049	
1050	> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
1051	> "Deadlock needs two locks taken in different orders. Here I only ever hold ONE lock — the machine lock — and there's no nested second lock, so there's no ordering cycle. The re-entry is the same thread re-taking the same `RLock`, which is safe by definition."
1052	
1053	> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
1054	> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is tiny and there's only one machine, so grabbing a short-held lock is simpler than a retry loop. For a shared-inventory distributed case I'd flip to optimistic — a DB compare-and-set — because you can't hold a Python lock across processes."
1055	
1056	> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-transaction?"
1057	> "In-process, a crash takes the whole process down, so the half-done state dies with it — no orphaned lock. In the DB version the compare-and-set is a single atomic statement, so it either committed fully or not at all."
1058	
1059	❓ **If asked — the thread-safe version:**
1060	
1061	**Part A — the hook + subclass:**
1062	```python copy
1063	# ─── §6 core (clean): no-op hook, zero lock code in the hot path ───
1064	@contextmanager
1065	def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
1066	    yield
1067	
1068	# ─── §9 thread-safe subclass: overrides ONLY the hook ───
1069	class ThreadSafeVendingService(VendingService):
1070	    def __init__(self, slots: dict[str, Slot], coin_bank: CoinBank) -> None:
1071	        super().__init__(slots, coin_bank)
1072	        self._machine_lock = threading.RLock()      # RLock: a guarded method may re-enter
1073	
1074	    @contextmanager
1075	    def _lock(self) -> Iterator[None]:
1076	        with self._machine_lock:
1077	            yield

1080	**Part B — the modified method.** The public `dispense` body is **identical** between §6 and §9 — that's the whole point of the Template-Method hook:
